games.explanation: Shane Baz gets a reputation he doesn't quite deserve against righty-heavy lineups, and the Athletics are bringing exactly the wrong kind of bats for him to dance around. Against right-handed hitters he's coughing up a .329 xwOBA with barrels in the double digits, and Kurtz, Soderstrom, and Rooker all profile as the kind of hard contact that turns his command lapses into damage. Civale isn't a star, but he doesn't have to be when the opposing arm is the one walking 10 percent of the batters he faces. At plus money on the side the math actually likes, the price is doing the work for you.

Chase Burns has been a buzzsaw against righties and a piñata against lefties, which is a problem when you have to face Yordan Alvarez in a bandbox. The split is stark enough to matter on its own, but Burns has been walking lefties at nearly a 12% clip while getting barreled north of 11%, and Houston's order is built to exploit exactly that. Arrighetti is no prize, but the simulator sees a 5.5 to 5.3 game in a park that plays to the dog's power, and the market is pricing this like the Astros are road underdogs by default. At +140, you're getting a coin flip dressed up as a longshot.

The market is selling Tampa as a road dog because Boston is at home, not because the pitching matchup actually favors the Red Sox. Payton Tolle has walked over ten percent of the hitters he's faced and gets bullied by righties, which is awkward when the visiting lineup runs Diaz, Caminero, and Aranda at him in order. Nick Martinez doesn't miss bats but he doesn't give them away either, and that profile plays fine against a Boston lineup that strikes out plenty on its own. Tampa is the better team by a real margin here (Elo has it as roughly an 80-point gap) and they're priced like the worse one. +130 on the side with the cleaner pitcher and the scarier top of the order is the bet.

Janson Junk has spent the year letting righties pound him into the ground, which is inconvenient when the lineup across the diamond is built almost entirely of right-handed bats who hit the ball loud. James Wood in particular fits the profile that gives Junk fits, and the supporting cast around him puts up enough hard contact to turn a starter with a 47% hard-hit rate into a problem by the third inning. Littell isn't an ace, but he doesn't need to be in a projected coin-flip game where the simulator has both sides scratching for the same 4.6 runs. Getting plus money on the better team in a tossup is the entire job, and +135 covers a lot of variance.

Joe Ryan's surface line looks like an ace, but the cracks are exactly where Cleveland is built to attack. Ryan gives up loud contact when he misses (a barrel rate north of 11% tells the story), and Ramírez and Manzardo are the kind of bats that punish the mistake pitches rather than chase the good ones. Bibee is the steadier arm tonight, the wind is howling straight in to take the cheap homer off the board, and Minnesota's offense outside Buxton thins out fast. Getting the better team at a near coin-flip price is the whole point.

Jack Leiter's surface numbers look like a serviceable young arm until you notice he's still walking nearly 11% of the hitters he sees, and the Cubs are exactly the patient, contact-heavy lineup built to make him pay for it. A free-pass rate that high against a Bregman-Happ-Hoerner core means traffic every inning, and Cabrera quietly brings the better strikeout stuff and the cleaner xwOBA to the other dugout. Texas leans on Seager and not much else once you get past him, which is a thin offense to bet on against a righty missing bats at a 28% clip. The market sees a road favorite and shaves the number; we see the better team, the better starter, and the lineup that punishes wildness. -138 is a fair toll for the side that should be closer to -160.

Randy Vasquez is the kind of pitcher whose ERA flatters him until you notice he can't miss bats and walks the ballpark, and the Cardinals get to swing against him as plus money. The Padres are paying him to navigate a lineup where Herrera mashes anything resembling his profile, and Vasquez's 13.8% strikeout rate means contact is coming whether San Diego likes it or not. Dustin May isn't a star but he misses enough bats to keep this honest, and the sims have it as a coin flip the market is pricing as a blowout. At +125 on the better roster facing the worse arm, you take the dog and let the at-bats sort it out.

picks.explanation: Toronto is the better team and Yesavage is the better arm, but a rookie who walks nearly 13% of righties he sees doesn't get to dictate a 1.5-run blowout against a lineup with Trout and Neto stacked at the top. Yesavage has been a different pitcher against right-handed bats, surrendering a .332 xwOBA with the kind of barrel rates that turn 4-1 games into 4-3 games in a hurry. Kochanowicz is bad enough to keep this from being a laugher in either direction, which is exactly what a runline buyer wants: traffic, walks, a roof game where the ball carries just enough. The market is pricing this like Toronto wins by three, and the projected margin is closer to one. +1.5 at this number is the rookie tax nobody else is collecting.
